mappings.rationaleAI: The cult of Mithras as a mystery religion in the Roman Empire with possible Zoroastrian connections directly addresses Mithraism (B.5) and mystery religions (E.1).
Zoroastrianism covers the cult of Ahura Mazda and its influence on Zoroaster's preaching (B.4), and Zurvanism as a related theological development (B.6).

The Epic of Gilgamesh as ancient Mesopotamian literature covers themes of mortality, the flood narrative predating Genesis, and Mesopotamian religious beliefs — directly relevant to Mesopotamian religions (A.2).
Gnosticism covers the dualist cosmology, the concept of gnosis, the Nag Hammadi library, and its relationship to Judaism, Platonism, and Christianity — a precise match to Gnosticism (E.2).
Greek mythology covers the Olympian pantheon, creation myths, hero narratives, and the social functions of myth in religion — directly relevant to Greek mythology (C.2) and the Greek pantheon (C.3).

Akhenaten's religious reforms centred on Aten worship, abandonment of traditional polytheism, and debates about monotheism directly address the Egyptian pantheon (A.3.c) and major forms of Egyptian religion (A.3.d).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
mappings.rationaleAI: The Egyptian Book of the Dead as funerary texts covering the afterlife, Osiris, ka and ba concepts directly addresses Egyptian religious literature (A.3.b) and religious symbolism and iconography (A.3.e).
Norse mythology as the pre-Christian religion of the Germanic/Scandinavian peoples directly addresses religion of the Germanic peoples (G.2).

Etruscan religious practices including haruspicy (divination from animal entrails) and tomb paintings reflect Roman religion's precursors, relevant to Roman worship, practices, and institutions (D.3) given Etruscan influence on Roman religion.

Stoicism as one of the most influential philosophies of the ancient Hellenistic world, covering its ethics, physics, and influence on Christianity — directly relevant to Hellenistic religious philosophies (E.4).
Epicureanism as a Hellenistic philosophical school addressing the rejection of fear of gods, atomism, and its challenge to Stoicism — directly relevant to Hellenistic religious philosophies (E.4).
Neoplatonism covers emanation metaphysics, contemplative ascent, and its influence on Christian theology and Islamic philosophy — directly relevant to Hellenistic religious philosophies (E.4).
The Celts covers Druidism and religious practices of Celtic peoples — directly relevant to religion of the Celts (G.1).
The Druids as priestly elite of Celtic societies including their roles as priests, judges, and the question of human sacrifice — directly relevant to religion of the Celts (G.1).
The oath in the classical world with gods as guarantors covers the religious dimension of Greek (C.4) and Roman (D.3) worship, practices, and institutions.
The Trojan War as central to Greek mythology and identity, including the role of gods in the narrative — relevant to Greek mythology and religious literature (C.2).
Homer's Iliad depicts the relationship between gods and mortals as a central theme — directly relevant to Greek mythology (C.2) and the Greek pantheon (C.3).
Thebes in Greek religion includes Dionysus's birthplace and Cadmus's founding myth — directly relevant to Greek religion's historical development (C.1) and mythology (C.2).
The Delphic Oracle as the most prestigious oracular shrine covers Greek worship practices and institutions (C.4), and divination relates to quasi-scientific cults (E.5).
Paganism in the Renaissance covers the revival of Neoplatonic and Hermetic texts and the concept of prisca theologia — relevant to the legacy of Hellenistic religious philosophies (E.4).
The Hittites as an Anatolian power with their own religious traditions and cuneiform archives — relevant to religions of the ancient peoples of Asia Minor (A.4).
The Phoenicians as Canaanite people with their role as cultural intermediaries — relevant to Syrian and Palestinian religions (A.5).
The Tale of Sinuhe as Middle Kingdom Egyptian literature illuminating Egyptian religious themes of death, loyalty, and the afterlife — relevant to Egyptian religious literature and mythology (A.3.b).
The Shahnameh preserves Iranian mythology from creation to the Arab conquest — relevant to general characteristics of Iranian religions (B.1).
The Mabinogion's Celtic mythology including pre-Christian origins, the otherworld, and sovereignty themes — directly relevant to religion of the Celts (G.1).
The Amazons as figures in Greek mythology with connections to Scythian warrior women relate to Greek mythology (C.2) and Scythian religion (F.1).
